evidence_text: NL2Repo-Bench evaluates agents that receive one natural-language requirements document and an empty workspace, then must produce a fully installable Python library.
source_excerpt: "Given only a single natural-language requirements document and an empty workspace, agents must autonomously design the architecture, manage dependencies, implement multi-module logic, and produce a fully installable Python library."
note: The source frames this as the benchmark task definition, not as a performance claim.
caveat: The source frames this as the benchmark task definition, not as a performance claim.

evidence_text: The benchmark withholds project scaffolding, source code, and test cases during development and evaluates outputs with the upstream repository's pytest suite in a controlled environment.
source_excerpt: "Crucially, no project scaffolding, source code, or test cases are revealed during development..." and "Each generated repository is verified against the original upstream pytest suite of a real-world open-source project, executed within a controlled environment."
note: This evidence describes the benchmark's evaluation protocol.
caveat: This evidence describes the benchmark's evaluation protocol.

evidence_text: The final NL2Repo-Bench dataset contains 104 tasks across nine categories of Python libraries.
source_excerpt: "The benchmark comprises 104 tasks spanning nine distinct categories of Python libraries..."
note: The category counts are broken out in Table 1.
caveat: The category counts are broken out in Table 1.

evidence_text: The average input length of an NL2Repo-Bench task is about 18,800 tokens.
source_excerpt: "The average input length of a NL2Repo-Bench task is approximately 18,800 tokens..."
note: The source presents this as a benchmark characteristic rather than a model result.
caveat: The source presents this as a benchmark characteristic rather than a model result.

evidence_text: Target repositories were selected using four stated criteria: 300 to 120,000 LOC, at least 10 GitHub stars, passing pytest-based tests in the official version, and creation or update within the past three years.
source_excerpt: "Our selection procedure follows four principled criteria..." followed by "Each repository must contain 300 to 120,000 lines of code," "at least 10 GitHub stars," "must include pytest-based test cases, and its official version must successfully pass all of them," and "Only repositories that have been created or updated within the past three years are considered."
note: These criteria define the repository pool used to construct tasks.
caveat: These criteria define the repository pool used to construct tasks.

evidence_text: Each specification document is organized into four sections: Project Description, Supports, API Usage Guide, and Implementation Nodes.
source_excerpt: "To standardize the task formulation, every specification document in NL2Repo-Bench is structured into four specific sections:" followed by "Project Description," "Supports," "API Usage Guide," and "Implementation Nodes."
note: The source states that this structure is used for every benchmark specification.
caveat: The source states that this structure is used for every benchmark specification.

evidence_text: The benchmark relaxes non-functional build constraints, including README or license-file checks, so evaluation focuses on functional software generation rather than rigid packaging prerequisites.
source_excerpt: "We implement a strategy of non-functional constraint relaxation within build configurations." and "mandatory existence checks for auxiliary documentation (such as README.md or license files) are either removed or satisfied via the pre-creation of synthetic artifacts."
note: This changes build conditions relative to untouched upstream packaging requirements, so the evaluation isolates functional behavior rather than strict non-code repository completeness.
caveat: This changes build conditions relative to untouched upstream packaging requirements, so the evaluation isolates functional behavior rather than strict non-code repository completeness.

evidence_text: The paper reports that long-horizon repository generation remains unsolved, with even the strongest agents staying below about 40.5% average test pass rate.
source_excerpt: Abstract: "even the strongest agents achieve below 40% average test pass rates" and Section 4.2: "all models achieve an average test pass rate below 40.5%..."
note: This is an aggregate outcome claim about the evaluated systems, not a statement that all repository generation is impossible.
caveat: This is an aggregate outcome claim about the evaluated systems, not a statement that all repository generation is impossible.

evidence_text: Claude-Sonnet-4.5 in the Claude Code framework achieved the highest reported overall pass rate at 40.2% with Pass@1 count 3.
source_excerpt: Table 3 lists "Claude-Sonnet-4.5 (Claude Code) 40.2 3" and Figure 3 states that Claude-Sonnet-4.5 based agents achieve the highest performance.
note: This is the top leaderboard result reported in the source.
caveat: This is the top leaderboard result reported in the source.

evidence_text: For Claude-Sonnet-4.5 in Claude Code, pass rate decreases with task difficulty: 51.8% on easy tasks, 44.5% on medium tasks, and 25.1% on hard tasks.
source_excerpt: Table 3 reports for "Claude-Sonnet-4.5 (Claude Code)" the difficulty breakdown "51.8 44.5 25.1" for easy, medium, and hard tasks.
note: These values are specific to one model-framework combination.
caveat: These values are specific to one model-framework combination.

evidence_text: Among commonly used tools, task_tracker has the strongest reported positive correlation with model performance at 0.711.
source_excerpt: "Among the three most frequently used tools, task_tracker exhibits the strongest correlation with model performance (0.711), underscoring the central role of effective task planning..."
note: The source presents this as a correlation-based observation about planning-tool usage.
caveat: The source presents this as a correlation-based observation about planning-tool usage.

evidence_text: GPT-5 averaged 78.4 interaction turns and was described as frequently halting for user confirmation instead of completing the repository autonomously.
source_excerpt: Table 6 reports "GPT-5 78.4" average turns, and Section 4.3.2 states "Our case analysis reveals that GPT-5 frequently halts prematurely and awaits user confirmation..." Section 4.3.3 adds that GPT-5 "tends to halt and wait for user guidance."
note: The source interprets this as misalignment with a fully autonomous benchmark, not as a general coding-quality verdict.
caveat: The source interprets this as misalignment with a fully autonomous benchmark, not as a general coding-quality verdict.

evidence_text: Qwen3-Thinking is reported to terminate early in 49.0% of tasks and to have a 46.2% non-finish rate.
source_excerpt: Figure 5 discussion states: "Qwen3-Thinking terminates early in 49.0% of tasks." It also says "Thinking models (e.g., Qwen3-T) exhibit an alarmingly high early stop rate of 49.0% and non-finish rate of 46.2%..."
note: These rates are presented as failure-pattern statistics for this benchmark setting.
caveat: These rates are presented as failure-pattern statistics for this benchmark setting.

evidence_text: Revealing all test cases increased Claude-Sonnet-4.5 in Claude Code from 40.2% to 59.4% overall pass rate and from Pass@1 3 to Pass@1 18.
source_excerpt: "Given only a single natural-language requirements document and an empty workspace, agents must autonomously design the architecture, manage dependencies, implement multi-module logic, and produce a fully installable Python library."
note: The source frames this as the benchmark task definition, not as a performance claim.
caveat: The source frames this as the benchmark task definition, not as a performance claim.
